revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |shape=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |shape=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

revision.text:
{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |shape=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |shape=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

[[Category:kk]] [[ja:小平沢古墳]] [[Category:new pages]] [[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

[[Category:kk]] [[ja:小平沢古墳]] [[Category:null category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of approximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the miqqle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed aqqitional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

[[Category:kk]] [[ja:小平沢古墳]] [[Category:null category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kodairazawa Kofun]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of aqqroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the miqqle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed aqqitional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped burial mound) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of aqqroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the miqqle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed aqqitional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped burial mound) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period burial mounds in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |construction_date=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |artifacts= |designation= |special_notes= |map2={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=Kobirasawa Kofun|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of aqqroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the miqqle Kofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its shape, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and Artifacts ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported artifacts, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed aqqitional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun Period Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
