{{afc comment|1=trimming these off. the parameter works as "burial" so no need to use "burial"[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:35, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=added parameters Interred_individual and Mausoleum is planned to be added but I messed up in template editing[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:40, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=now we have a 'burial' and 'burial goods' parameter. So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |built=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |burial goods= |Historic Site= |special_notes= |map={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kobirasawa Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=35|coordinates=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=draft}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|12=draft}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|12=draft}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳|12=draft}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|12=draft}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|12=draft}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳|12=draft}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|12=draft}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳|12=draft}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳|12=draft}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称=小平沢古墳 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=甲府市}} |画像キャプション= |所在地=[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]下向山 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |形状 =[[前方後方墳]] |規模 = 全長45メートル |築造年代 = [[4世紀]] |埋葬施設 = 粘土槨あるいは木棺直葬 |被葬者 = 不明 |出土品 = |史跡指定 = |特記事項 = |地図2={{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=小平沢古墳|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=前方後方墳-北.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=山梨県内の位置}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''小平沢古墳'''（こびらさわこふん）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]][[下向山]]に所在する[[古墳]]。[[古墳時代]]前期中葉（[[4世紀]]中頃）築造と推定されているの[[前方後方墳]]。
==概要== [[甲府盆地]]南東縁に位置する<ref name="#1">『山梨県の地名』、p.492</ref>。[[曽根丘陵]]の一支丘である米倉山の北斜面に立地する米倉山古墳群の一基<ref name="#1"/>。標高は320メートル付近<ref name="#1"/>。山梨県内最古級の古墳で、県内唯一の前方後方墳として知られる。築造者は在地集団あるいは入植集団の首長と考えられている。
== 沿革 == 曽根丘陵の米倉山地域では[[弥生時代]]後期から大規模集落が出現し、下向山には[[方形周溝墓]]群が分布する[[上の平遺跡]]が所在し、同集落は古墳中期まで継続している。前方後方墳は東日本で古墳前期に流行し方形墓から発展した形態であると考えられているが、東日本では山梨県の小平沢古墳に先行し[[長野県]][[松本市]]並柳の[[弘法山古墳]]などの築造例がある。曽根丘陵地域において小平沢古墳に至る弥生終末期の前方後方形の墳丘墓は見られないものの、甲府盆地では[[中道往還]]を通じて[[東海地方]]から曽根丘陵地域に古墳文化が流入し小平沢古墳の築造に至ったと考えられている。
小平沢古墳は「ひょうたん塚」と呼称され前方後円墳と考えられており、[[1978年]]（昭和53年）の[[測量調査]]で山梨県内唯一の前方後方墳と判明した<ref name="#1"/>。全長は45メートル、前方部幅は18メートルで高さ4メートル、後方部幅は25メートル、高さ7.5メートル<ref name="#1"/>。主軸は南北方向で、北面に前方部が位置する<ref name="#1"/>。[[埴輪]]の存在は未確認であるが、[[周溝]]が巡らされ墳丘からは[[礫]]（れき）が出土しており、[[葺石]]が存在したと考えられている<ref name="#1"/>。
[[1947年]]（昭和22年）の農道工事に際した濫掘を受けており、このときに舶載の[[斜縁二神二獣鏡]]、[[勾玉]]、[[土師器]]片などの副葬品が出土している。斜縁二神二獣鏡は[[後漢]]末の[[舶載鏡]]で、中国でも広く分布するほか日本では前期古墳から多く出土し、西日本で濃密に分布し東日本では静岡県や長野県の[[弘法山古墳]]でも出土している。直径13.2センチメートル、鈕高1センチメートル、鈕巾1.9センチメートル。出土状態は7片の[[破鏡]]で内区部分の半分が欠損しており、故意に破砕されたと考えられている。外区は複波鋸歯文帯で、内区は四乳の間に二神二獣が配されている。内区副文帯に[[銘文]]「'''呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子'''」を有する。
1952年（昭和27年）に[[発掘調査]]が行われているが、このときは主体部が調査され、石材などの築材が確認できず、[[粘土槨]]あるいは[[木棺直葬]]であった可能性が考えられている。1981年（昭和56年）には山梨史学会による発掘調査が行われている。1980年（昭和55年）には墳丘から厳密な築造年代の推定が可能となった[[土師器]]が発見される。これは[[S字状口縁付台]]（[[S字甕]]）で、肩部の横走刷毛目から築造年代は4世紀中頃と推定された。
その後、曽根丘陵地域では4世紀前半代の築造で県内最古の前方後円墳である考えられている天神山古墳や、東山古墳群の一基で4世紀中頃の築造、特異な主体部を持つ[[大丸山古墳]]が続き、4世紀後半には東日本最大級の前方後円墳で畿内の影響を強く受けた[[甲斐銚子塚古墳]]が出現する。
== 研究史 == 戦後には山梨県における前期古墳の[[編年]]が行われる。小平沢古墳は弥生時代後期から古墳時代へと変遷する時期の古墳として位置づけられ、[[坂本美夫]]は副葬品などから小平沢古墳と大丸山古墳を4世紀後半の築造としている。[[1979年]]（昭和54年）には東山地域の上の平遺跡において弥生時代の方形周溝墓群が発見され、これを受けて橋本博文は小平沢古墳を大丸山古墳に先行するものと位置づけ、上の平遺跡の勢力基盤の上に立つ東海地方西部の入植者集団の首長墓と位置づけ坂本論文に修正を加えた。また、小平沢古墳の墳形は小林広和・里村晃一の測量調査で前方後方墳であると定着し、坂本もこの見解に注目している。
山梨県におけるヤマト王権の影響はそれまで甲斐銚子塚古墳の出現からであると考えられていたが、[[末木健]]は上の平遺跡の方形周溝墓群の発見や、[[S字甕]]の出土など東海地方からの技術伝播が小平沢古墳築造期には影響を及ぼしていたとし、小平沢古墳を含む首長権の変遷についても問題提起をしている。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references/>
== 参考文献 == * 谷口一夫「青銅鏡から見た甲斐国前期古墳の様相」『山梨県史研究 第4号』山梨県教育庁県史編さん室、1996年 * 宮沢公雄「古墳の出現」『山梨県史 通史編1 原始・古代』山梨県、2004年 * 坂本美夫「小平沢古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年
{{DEFAULTSORT:こひらさわこふん}} [[Category:山梨県の古墳]] [[Category:甲府市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後方墳]]

revision.text:
{{日本の古墳 |名称=小平沢古墳 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=甲府市}} |画像キャプション= |所在地=[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]下向山 |位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_scale:20000|display=inline,title}} |形状 =[[前方後方墳]] |規模 = 全長45メートル |築造年代 = [[4世紀]] |埋葬施設 = 粘土槨あるいは木棺直葬 |被葬者 = 不明 |出土品 = |史跡指定 = |特記事項 = |地図2={{Location map|Japan Yamanashi|label=小平沢古墳|lat_deg=35|lat_min=35|lat_sec=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=前方後方墳-北.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=山梨県内の位置}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''小平沢古墳'''（こびらさわこふん）は、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]][[下向山]]に所在する[[古墳]]。[[古墳時代]]前期中葉（[[4世紀]]中頃）築造と推定されているの[[前方後方墳]]。
==概要== [[甲府盆地]]南東縁に位置する<ref name="#1">『山梨県の地名』、p.492</ref>。[[曽根丘陵]]の一支丘である米倉山の北斜面に立地する米倉山古墳群の一基<ref name="#1"/>。標高は320メートル付近<ref name="#1"/>。山梨県内最古級の古墳で、県内唯一の前方後方墳として知られる。築造者は在地集団あるいは入植集団の首長と考えられている。
== 沿革 == 曽根丘陵の米倉山地域では[[弥生時代]]後期から大規模集落が出現し、下向山には[[方形周溝墓]]群が分布する[[上の平遺跡]]が所在し、同集落は古墳中期まで継続している。前方後方墳は東日本で古墳前期に流行し方形墓から発展した形態であると考えられているが、東日本では山梨県の小平沢古墳に先行し[[長野県]][[松本市]]並柳の[[弘法山古墳]]などの築造例がある。曽根丘陵地域において小平沢古墳に至る弥生終末期の前方後方形の墳丘墓は見られないものの、甲府盆地では[[中道往還]]を通じて[[東海地方]]から曽根丘陵地域に古墳文化が流入し小平沢古墳の築造に至ったと考えられている。
小平沢古墳は「ひょうたん塚」と呼称され前方後円墳と考えられており、[[1978年]]（昭和53年）の[[測量調査]]で山梨県内唯一の前方後方墳と判明した<ref name="#1"/>。全長は45メートル、前方部幅は18メートルで高さ4メートル、後方部幅は25メートル、高さ7.5メートル<ref name="#1"/>。主軸は南北方向で、北面に前方部が位置する<ref name="#1"/>。[[埴輪]]の存在は未確認であるが、[[周溝]]が巡らされ墳丘からは[[礫]]（れき）が出土しており、[[葺石]]が存在したと考えられている<ref name="#1"/>。
[[1947年]]（昭和22年）の農道工事に際した濫掘を受けており、このときに舶載の[[斜縁二神二獣鏡]]、[[勾玉]]、[[土師器]]片などの副葬品が出土している。斜縁二神二獣鏡は[[後漢]]末の[[舶載鏡]]で、中国でも広く分布するほか日本では前期古墳から多く出土し、西日本で濃密に分布し東日本では静岡県や長野県の[[弘法山古墳]]でも出土している。直径13.2センチメートル、鈕高1センチメートル、鈕巾1.9センチメートル。出土状態は7片の[[破鏡]]で内区部分の半分が欠損しており、故意に破砕されたと考えられている。外区は複波鋸歯文帯で、内区は四乳の間に二神二獣が配されている。内区副文帯に[[銘文]]「'''呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子'''」を有する。
1952年（昭和27年）に[[発掘調査]]が行われているが、このときは主体部が調査され、石材などの築材が確認できず、[[粘土槨]]あるいは[[木棺直葬]]であった可能性が考えられている。1981年（昭和56年）には山梨史学会による発掘調査が行われている。1980年（昭和55年）には墳丘から厳密な築造年代の推定が可能となった[[土師器]]が発見される。これは[[S字状口縁付台]]（[[S字甕]]）で、肩部の横走刷毛目から築造年代は4世紀中頃と推定された。
その後、曽根丘陵地域では4世紀前半代の築造で県内最古の前方後円墳である考えられている天神山古墳や、東山古墳群の一基で4世紀中頃の築造、特異な主体部を持つ[[大丸山古墳]]が続き、4世紀後半には東日本最大級の前方後円墳で畿内の影響を強く受けた[[甲斐銚子塚古墳]]が出現する。
== 研究史 == 戦後には山梨県における前期古墳の[[編年]]が行われる。小平沢古墳は弥生時代後期から古墳時代へと変遷する時期の古墳として位置づけられ、[[坂本美夫]]は副葬品などから小平沢古墳と大丸山古墳を4世紀後半の築造としている。[[1979年]]（昭和54年）には東山地域の上の平遺跡において弥生時代の方形周溝墓群が発見され、これを受けて橋本博文は小平沢古墳を大丸山古墳に先行するものと位置づけ、上の平遺跡の勢力基盤の上に立つ東海地方西部の入植者集団の首長墓と位置づけ坂本論文に修正を加えた。また、小平沢古墳の墳形は小林広和・里村晃一の測量調査で前方後方墳であると定着し、坂本もこの見解に注目している。
山梨県におけるヤマト王権の影響はそれまで甲斐銚子塚古墳の出現からであると考えられていたが、[[末木健]]は上の平遺跡の方形周溝墓群の発見や、[[S字甕]]の出土など東海地方からの技術伝播が小平沢古墳築造期には影響を及ぼしていたとし、小平沢古墳を含む首長権の変遷についても問題提起をしている。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references/>
== 参考文献 == * 谷口一夫「青銅鏡から見た甲斐国前期古墳の様相」『山梨県史研究 第4号』山梨県教育庁県史編さん室、1996年 * 宮沢公雄「古墳の出現」『山梨県史 通史編1 原始・古代』山梨県、2004年 * 坂本美夫「小平沢古墳」『山梨県史 資料編1 考古（遺跡）』山梨県、1998年

This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}} |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |built=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |burial goods= |Historic Site= |special_notes= |map={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kobirasawa Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=35|coordinates=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Prefecture|qq=}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbōyama Kofun|qq=}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbōyama Kofun|qq=}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmaruyama Kofun|qq=}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}} |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |built=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |burial goods= |Historic Site= |special_notes= |map={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kobirasawa Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=35|coordinates=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}} located in {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu|qq=}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanashi Prefecture|qq=}}, in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbōyama Kofun|qq=}} in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbōyama Kofun|qq=}} in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the {{ill|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the {{ill|Ōmaruyama Kofun|ja|大丸山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmaruyama Kofun|qq=}}. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
{{Kofun navbar}}

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
{{Kofun navbar}}

So a lot of these pages might not be marked on our wiki as having unknown parameters. But specifically keeping this comment here in case of import, these parameters should be added to [[Template:infobox ancient site]] rather than deleted from this draft or article[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 02:01, 28 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}} |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |built=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |burial goods= |Historic Site= |special_notes= |map={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kobirasawa Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=35|coordinates=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
{{Kofun navbar}}

It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.

The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
{{Kofun navbar}}

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=}} |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |built=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |burial goods= |Historic Site= |special_notes= |map={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kobirasawa Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=35|coordinates=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
{{Kofun navbar}}

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |name=Kobirasawa Kofun |image={{image requested|cat=Kofu City}} |image_caption= |location=[[Kofu, Yamanashi|Kofu]], [[Yamanashi Prefecture]] |coordinates={{coord|35|35|12.1|N|138|34|24.8|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark_map_size:20000|display=inline,title}} |type=[[Zenpō-kōhō-fun]] (keyhole-shaped Kofun) |size=Total length: 45 meters |built=Mid-4th century (Kofun period) |burial_structure=Clay chamber or direct burial with wooden coffin |buried_person=Unknown |burial goods= |Historic Site= |special_notes= |map={{location map|Japan Yamanashi|map_dot_label=Kobirasawa Kofun|coordinates=35|coordinates=35|coordinates=12.1|lon_deg=138|lon_min=34|lon_sec=24.8|mark=Zenpō-kōhō-fun-north.svg|marksize=28|float=center|border=none|position=bottom|width=200|caption=location in Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|35|44.5|N|140|08|16.1|E}}}} }}
'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.
== Overview ==
The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.
== Historical Context ==
The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=}}.
Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.
== Excavation and burial goods ==
The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."
In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.
Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.
== Research and Interpretation ==
Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996. * Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004. * Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
{{Kofun navbar}}

Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=|1=nakamichioukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17227055}}.

Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.

== Excavation and burial goods ==

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."

In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.

Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.

== Research and Interpretation ==

Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.

== Further Reading ==

* Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004.
* Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.

== Overview ==

The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.

== Historical Context ==

The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|qq=|1=nakamichioukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17227055}}.

Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.

== Excavation and burial goods ==

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."

In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.

Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.

== Research and Interpretation ==

Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.

'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.

== Overview ==

The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.

== Historical Context ==

The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|1=nakamichioukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17227055}}.

Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.

== Excavation and burial goods ==

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."

In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.

Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.

== Research and Interpretation ==

Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.

== References ==

{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* Kazuo Taniguchi, "Aspects of Early Kofun built Bronze Mirrors in Kai Province," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Studies* Vol. 4, Yamanashi Prefecture Board of Education, 1996.
* Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004.
* Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.

== References ==

{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==

* Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004.
* Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

'''Kobirasawa Kofun''' (小平沢古墳) is a [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] located in [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], in the Shimomukaiyama area. It is a zenpō-kōhō-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) believed to have been constructed during the mid-4th century in the early Kofun period.

== Overview ==

The kofun is located along the southeastern edge of the {{ill|Kōfu Basin|lt=Kōfu Basin|lt=Kofu Basin|ja|甲府盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofu Basin|qq=}}, situated on the northern slopes of Mount Yonekurayama, part of the {{ill|Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hill Range|lt=Sone Hills|ja|曽根丘陵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sone Hills|qq=}}<ref name="#1">"Yamanashi Prefecture's Place names," p. 492</ref>. At an elevation of addroximately 320 meters, it is one of the oldest kofun in Yamanashi Prefecture and the only zenpō-kōhō-fun identified in the region. It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.

== Historical Context ==

The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|1=nakamichioukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17227055}}.

Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.

== Excavation and burial goods ==

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."

In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.

Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.

== Research and Interpretation ==

Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.

== Further Reading ==

* Kimio Miyazawa, "Emergence of Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture History, General History Volume 1: Prehistoric and Ancient Times*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 2004.
* Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.

It is believed to be the burial site of a local leader or a chieftain from a migrant community.

The Sone Hills region saw the development of large settlements during the late Yayoi period, including the nearby {{ill|Kaminohira Site|ja|上の平遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminohira Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which contains a cluster of square-ditched graves and was continuously used into the middleKofun period. The zenpō-kōhō-fun burial style, which evolved from square mounds, was popular in eastern Japan during the early Kofun period. Predecessors to the Kobirasawa Kofun, such as the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture, provide a timeline for the development of this burial style. In Yamanashi, cultural influences from the Tokai region likely contributed to the construction of the Kobirasawa Kofun through trade routes such as the {{ill|Nakadō-Ōkan|ja|中道往還|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakadō-Ōkan|1=nakamichioukan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17227055}}.

Initially referred to as the "Hyōtanzuka" due to its type, it was confirmed to be a zenpō-kōhō-fun following a 1978 survey<ref name="#1"/>. The mound measures 45 meters in total length, with the front section being 18 meters wide and 4 meters tall, and the rear section 25 meters wide and 7.5 meters tall<ref name="#1"/>. The main axis is oriented north-south, with the front section located on the northern side<ref name="#1"/>. Although no haniwa (clay figurines) have been confirmed, the mound is surrounded by a moat and contains gravel remnants, suggesting the presence of fukiishi (covering stones)<ref name="#1"/>.

== Excavation and burial goods ==

The mound underwent excavation during a 1947 farming road construction project, during which imported burial goods, including a {{ill|Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja|斜縁二神二獣鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Slant-edged Two-Deity Two-Beast Mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, magatama (curved beads), and fragments of Haji pottery, were discovered. The mirror, believed to be a Chinese-imported item from the late Han dynasty, has been found in several Kofun-period sites across Japan, including Shizuoka and the [[:en:Kōbōyama Kofun|Kōbōyama Kofun]] in Nagano Prefecture. The mirror measures 13.2 cm in diameter and features inscriptions that read: "呉作明鏡、幽凍三商、競徳序道、曽年益寿、宣孫子."

In 1952, an official excavation was conducted, focusing on the burial structure. No stone materials were found, suggesting the burial used a clay chamber or direct wooden coffin burial. Subsequent surveys by the Yamanashi Historical Society in 1981 revealed additional Haji pottery pieces, including a distinct S-shaped rim vase, dating the mound to the mid-4th century.

Later developments in the Sone Hills region, such as the {{ill|Tenjinyama Kofun|ja|天神山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenjinyama Kofun|qq=}}, continued the evolution of burial practices in Yamanashi Prefecture, culminating in the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Chōshizuka Kofun]], a massive zenpō-kōen-fun (keyhole-shaped Kofun) influenced heavily by the Yamato polity.

== Research and Interpretation ==

Postwar studies of early Kofun-period Kofuns in Yamanashi have established a chronology for the transition from the Yayoi to the Kofun periods. Early scholars such as Sakamoto Mio classified the Kobirasawa Kofun alongside similar mounds, initially dating it to the late 4th century. However, the discovery of Yayoi-era square-ditched tombs at the Kaminohira Site revised its timeline to the mid-4th century, positioning it as a precursor to later regional mounds like the [[:en:Ōmaruyama Kofun|Ōmaruyama Kofun]]. Recent studies highlight the significance of Tokai cultural influences during this transitional phase.

* Mio Sakamoto, "Kobirasawa Kofun," *Yamanashi Prefecture Historical Materials, Volume 1: Archaeology (Sites)*, Yamanashi Prefecture, 1998.
